choices.text: Semantic colors adapt automatically across modes and accessibility settings. `UIColor.label` = black in light mode + near-white in dark mode + adjusts for Increase Contrast / Differentiate Without Color settings — same code, four different actual colors depending on user context. Hardcoded `UIColor.black` = black always; on dark mode background = invisible; on Increase Contrast = doesn't get the boost; ignores user preferences. Apple defines semantic colors specifically so apps inherit dark mode + accessibility for free. Same pattern: `UIColor.systemBackground` over `UIColor.white`; `UIColor.secondaryLabel` over a specific gray. The discipline: think in terms of MEANING (primary text, background, secondary label) not absolute color values. Asset Catalog colors with light/dark variants achieve similar for brand colors. The rule: if a color has meaning beyond 'this specific RGB value,' use a semantic color or a mode-adapting custom color. Hardcoded RGB is for very specific decoration cases (a brand-color stripe that's always the same RGB regardless of mode), and even those benefit from being defined once and named.

questions.explanation: Semantic colors adapt automatically: dark mode + accessibility + Increase Contrast. Hardcoded RGB doesn't.

questions.prompt: Designer applies your brand accent color to text, backgrounds, borders, and accents — everywhere. Result?

choices.text: Brand loses meaning + design feels cluttered. iOS's convention: accent color signals INTERACTIVITY — appears on interactive elements (buttons, links, switch on-state, selected items) so users learn 'colored = tappable.' Spreading accent to backgrounds, decoration, body text dilutes the signal; user can no longer distinguish 'this is a tappable button' from 'this is just colored decoration.' Plus: heavy color fatigues the eye; chrome should be calm so content can stand out (deference principle from Module 1). Better pattern: one accent color, applied only to interactive elements, leaving 90%+ of the UI in grayscale + system colors. Apple's own apps demonstrate: Notes is mostly white/gray; Reminders is mostly white/gray; the accent appears in checkmarks, tab bar selected items, links, action buttons. The brand expression isn't in volume of color — it's in consistent, intentional application of accent for a single meaning. Apps that try to brand harder (color everywhere) usually feel less polished, not more. Restraint is the discipline.

questions.explanation: Loses 'accent = interactive' signal; cluttered. Accent ONLY on interactive elements; rest grayscale + system colors.
